1: 'These are the ordinances (mishpatim) you are to set before them' — the mishpatim (Ex Exodus 21:1-23:33, the Book of the Covenant) are the case-law applications of the Decalogue principles. Mishpat (H4941, judgment/ordinance) is the standard Hebrew term for legal decisions — these are the precedent rulings of a divine court, not arbitrary commands. The phrase 'set before them' suggests presentation for study and internalization, not mere imposition. The Book of the Covenant is the oldest legal code in Israel, and it shares structural parallels with other ANE law codes (Hammurabi, Lipit-Ishtar, the Hittite laws) — using the same conditional case-law form ('if a man does X, then Y') while often applying distinctly different principles.
2: The Hebrew slave law — a Hebrew debt-slave serves six years and goes free in the seventh year. The Sabbath principle is applied to human bondage: just as the land rests every seventh year (Ex Exodus 23:11) and the calendar has a weekly day of rest, so human servitude is capped at six years. This law addresses debt slavery (common in the ancient world), not chattel slavery (owning persons as property from birth). The maximum term of six years protects the covenant community's internal equality: Israelites are ultimately YHWH's servants, not each other's property in perpetuity.
3: If the slave came in single, he goes out single; if married, his wife goes with him. The law protects the family unit: the master cannot separate a pre-existing marriage to retain the wife as a slave when the husband goes free. The practical limitation of the master's power over the family reflects the covenant community's value of the family as prior to economic arrangements. The slave had a family before the debt; when the debt is cleared, the family is restored intact.
4: If the master gave the slave a wife and she bore sons and daughters, they remain the master's property. This provision is difficult by modern standards: children born during the term of slavery belong to the household in which they were born. The wife and children are the master's social investment. The next verse (Exodus 21:5-6) provides the alternative: the slave may choose permanent commitment and keep his family. The law provides no easy exit; the covenant relationship with the family is a binding obligation in both directions.
5: If the slave declares love for his master and does not wish to go free, the choice is presented formally — 'I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go free.' The voluntary permanent slave makes an explicit, witnessed declaration. The motivation is relational: love for the household, not economic desperation. This voluntary choice of servitude out of love is the background for the messianic servant passages and for the apostle Paul's self-description as a slave of Christ (Romans 1:1; Philippians 1:1) — the one who could be free by right chooses bondage out of love for the master and the household.
6: The ear-piercing at the doorpost — the slave is brought to the doorpost (mezuzah, the same word as the doorpost on which the Shema is affixed, Deuteronomy 6:9) and his ear is bored through with an awl. The doorpost marks the boundary of the household; the ear-piercing at the doorpost marks permanent belonging to that household. The ear is pierced because it is the organ of obedience: the slave who listens and obeys is marked as one who permanently belongs to the household of the listening. Psalms 40:6 ('ears you have pierced/opened for me') and Hebrews 10:5-7 apply this imagery christologically to the voluntary self-offering of the Son to the Father's will.
7: A man who sells his daughter as a servant — the female slave law differs because the daughter may be acquired as a potential wife (for the master or his son), not merely as a labor resource. This practice is regulated, not endorsed: the law immediately specifies protections against its abuse. The social context: in an economically precarious household, a father might sell a daughter to a more prosperous household both to relieve his own poverty and to secure a better future for the daughter (by marrying into a stable household). The law now regulates what happens once this arrangement exists.
8: If she does not please the master who designated her for himself, he must let her be redeemed — she cannot be sold to a foreign people. The prohibition against selling to foreigners is the first protection: she is an Israelite woman; her covenant membership limits how she can be disposed of. The right of redemption gives her male relatives (father, brothers) the ability to buy her out of the arrangement. The law is asymmetrical by modern standards but provided genuine protection in its ancient context — she is not merchandise.
9: If he designates her for his son, he shall deal with her as with a daughter — the female slave who becomes the son's wife acquires the full status of a daughter in the household. The son cannot treat her as a sub-wife or second-class wife; her status is elevated to family membership. The principle: the covenant protects the vulnerable from exploitation even within arrangements that carry inherent power asymmetries.
10: If the master takes another wife, he must not diminish the first wife's food, clothing, or marital rights. The law protects the existing wife against being deprioritized when a second wife enters the household. The three protected elements — food, clothing, conjugal rights — are the essential duties of a husband. Paul invokes a similar principle in 1 Corinthians 7:3-5. The law prevents a man from effectively abandoning his first wife by economic and relational neglect while remaining technically married to her.
11: If the master does not provide these three things for the female slave-wife, she goes out free without any payment — the master's failure of duty releases her from the arrangement without penalty. The law creates a bilateral obligation: the master's rights over the slave-wife are contingent on his fulfilling his duties as a husband. When he fails, her freedom is the consequence. This corrective freedom-grant is a form of the covenant's protection of the vulnerable: the power differential is checked by enforceable obligations.
12: 'Anyone who strikes a person so that they die must be put to death' — the capital homicide law. The theological grounding is in Genesis 9:6: human life is made in the image of God; its intentional destruction requires the life of the killer. The death penalty for murder is not arbitrary retributive violence but the acknowledgment of human dignity: to kill a human being is to assault the image of God. The law immediately qualifies this with the unintentional-homicide exception (Exodus 21:13) and the premeditated-murder rule (Exodus 21:14).
13: If the killing was not premeditated but God allowed it to happen, there is a place of refuge to which the killer may flee. The cities of refuge (developed in Num 35 and Deut 19) are established precisely on this principle: unintentional homicide is legally and morally distinct from murder. The killer who caused accidental death is protected from the avenger of blood (go'el hadam) by reaching a city of refuge. This distinction between murder and manslaughter is maintained in virtually all subsequent Western legal traditions.
14: If a man kills another person intentionally — 'with deliberate forethought' (b'ormah = with cunning/premeditation) — he is to be taken even from the altar and put to death. The altar was traditionally a place of sanctuary (cf. Adonijah and Joab in 1 Kings 1:50; Exodus 2:28-34). The exception states that premeditated murder cannot be sheltered even by the sacred precincts. Joab's death at the altar by Solomon's command is the fulfillment of this principle (1 Kings 2:28-34): deliberate murder forfeits the protection of the holy place.
15: Whoever strikes his father or mother shall be put to death — the fifth commandment (honor your parents) receives its maximum sanction. Physical assault on parents is a capital offense because the family is the foundational social institution of the covenant community. In ANE household law, violent insubordination to the paterfamilias was treated as an attack on the entire social order. The principle: a community that cannot maintain the basic relational structures within the family cannot maintain any other social structure either.
16: Anyone who kidnaps a person (gonev ish = steals a man) and sells them or is found with them must be put to death — the first explicit anti-trafficking law in ancient literature. The stealing of a human being as a commodity for sale is a capital crime. The law applies equally whether the trafficker has already sold the victim or is caught in possession — the mere act of trafficking human life carries the death penalty. This principle is absent from Hammurabi's Code, which regulated the slave trade without prohibiting it. The Mosaic law's capital penalty for kidnapping stands in sharp contrast.
17: Whoever curses his father or mother shall be put to death — the verbal counterpart to Exodus 21:15. Not merely physical assault but the pronouncement of a curse against parents is a capital offense. Qillel (to curse/dishonor) is the antonym of kabed (to honor/give weight to). The fifth commandment stands: honoring parents is not optional, and its opposite is not merely impolite but a crime against the covenant order. Jesus cites this verse to confront the Pharisees' use of qorban (dedicated offerings) as a way of avoiding the financial support of parents — technically legal but spiritually violating this command (Mark 7:10-13).
18: The quarrel injury law — if two men fight and one injures the other but not fatally, and the injured man recovers but is bedridden, the one who struck him is not charged with homicide but must pay for the injured man's lost time and ensure his complete recovery. The first explicit statement of tort liability in ancient law: you are financially responsible for the economic consequences of harm you caused to another person. The compensation covers the two heads of loss: lost income (shivto = his sitting/idleness) and medical expenses (rappo yirapeh = he shall surely cause him to be healed).
19: 'Only if the man gets up and walks around outside with the help of his staff will the one who struck him be free from liability — except that he must pay for the loss of his time and see that he is completely healed.' The standard for assessing compensation is recovery and mobility: once the injured man is able to walk with his staff, the primary obligation (full compensation for incapacitation) is discharged, though the attacker remains liable for the ongoing medical costs. This graduated liability anticipates the principle of proportionate compensation that governs all subsequent legal systems.
20: If a man beats his male or female slave with a rod and the slave dies under his hand, he must be punished. The killing of a slave by the master is not exempt from legal consequences — a significant departure from ancient standards in which slaves were typically the unprotected property of their masters. Whether the punishment was death (as for murder) or some lesser penalty is debated; the text says 'avenged (naqom yinaqem)' without specifying the mode. The principle is clear: the slave's life has legal standing; the master does not have unlimited power over it.
21: 'But if the slave survives a day or two, the master is not to be punished, for the slave is his money.' This provision acknowledges the economic reality of the slave relationship while providing minimal protection. The rationale that the slave's death represents the master's own economic loss is a recognition of the property relationship, not an endorsement of it. Modern readers rightly find this insufficient protection; the law is a minimum floor, not an ideal ceiling — it is working within an existing institution to limit its worst abuses rather than abolishing the institution entirely (which would happen gradually over the course of redemptive history).
22: If men are fighting and strike a pregnant woman so that she gives birth prematurely but there is no serious injury (ason), the man must pay what the woman's husband demands as assessed by the judges. The term ason (serious harm/injury) is the interpretive crux: if it refers to the mother, the law is about maternal injury; if to the premature infant, it raises questions about the legal status of the unborn. Ancient interpreters divided: the LXX rendered it as distinguishing a formed from an unformed fetus (implying different levels of legal protection); Talmudic interpretation focused primarily on the mother. The verse has been central in contemporary debates about abortion law.
23: But if there is serious injury (ason), then it is life for life — the lex talionis applies. The principle of exact proportionality: the punishment must match the crime precisely, neither more nor less. The lex talionis is often misread as a license for personal revenge; it is in fact a limitation on disproportionate retaliation. In ANE legal practice, punishments for the same offense often varied dramatically by the social status of the parties (a commoner injuring a nobleman might face death; the reverse might bring only a fine). The lex talionis equalizes: the same standard applies regardless of the parties' relative status.
24: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot — the fourfold list articulates the proportionality principle across different categories of injury. Archaeological evidence suggests these provisions were implemented through monetary compensation (the Talmud is explicit that financial equivalence was the practical application) rather than literal physical retaliation. Jesus addresses the lex talionis in Matthew 5:38-39 ('You have heard it said, an eye for an eye, but I say to you, do not resist the one who is evil') — he does not abolish the law's justice but transcends it by calling for non-retaliatory response. The kingdom ethic exceeds the legal minimum.
25: Burn for burn, wound for wound, stripe for stripe — the extension of the lex talionis to less severe injuries. Every category of harm has its proportionate consequence; no injury category is exempt from the proportionality principle. The comprehensive list signals that the principle is universal within the covenant community: all harm is subject to proportionate justice, from capital cases (life for life) down to welts and bruises (stripe for stripe). The law creates a total moral economy in which every harmful act has a proportionate cost.
26: If a master knocks out the eye of his male or female slave, he shall let the slave go free on account of the eye. The slave's body is not merely property; injury to the slave's physical person by the master triggers the slave's liberation. The lex talionis principle is adapted for the master-slave relationship: the master cannot lose his eye (that would equalize their social positions), so the slave's freedom compensates for the injury. The principle: the slave's physical integrity has legal standing that, when violated by the master, creates a legal claim. Freedom becomes the kofer (ransom payment) for the lost eye.
27: If a master knocks out a tooth of a male or female slave, the slave goes free for the tooth's sake — the same principle applied to a less severe injury. Even the loss of a single tooth by a slave at the master's hand is sufficient grounds for freedom. The protection of the slave's body is comprehensive: no injury is too minor to trigger the compensation-by-freedom mechanism. This is among the most socially protective provisions in any ancient law code regarding the treatment of enslaved persons by their owners.
28: The goring ox law — one of the most extensively discussed provisions in the Book of the Covenant. If an ox gores a person to death, the ox is stoned and its flesh not eaten. The ox is killed because it has shed human blood (cf. Genesis 9:5-6, which extends the blood-guilt principle to animals that kill humans). The flesh is not eaten because the animal has been the instrument of death and is contaminated by blood-guilt. The owner is not held criminally responsible if the ox had no history of dangerous behavior — the first incident establishes the standard of care.
29: If the ox had a history of goring and the owner was warned but did not secure the animal, and the ox then kills a person — both the ox and the owner die. The negligence principle: the owner knew of the risk, failed to act, and that failure caused a death. The owner's culpable negligence makes him a quasi-murderer; his life is forfeit. This principle of liability for foreseeable harm from a known dangerous condition runs through all subsequent Western tort law. The owner who knew and did nothing is morally equivalent to the one who acted with deliberate intent.
30: If the dead person's family accepts a ransom (kofer H3724), the owner may pay it instead of being executed. Kofer is the redemption price, from the root kapar (to cover/atone — the same root as yom kippur). A life can be ransomed by a monetary payment when the parties agree. This ransom principle runs through Israelite theology: the firstborn are ransomed (Ex Exodus 13:13-15), the census silver is ransom money (Ex Exodus 30:12), and the ultimate ransom of all is the substitutionary atonement of the Suffering Servant (Isaiah 53:10; Mark 10:45 — the Son of Man gives his life as a lutron, the Greek equivalent of kofer).
31: The goring ox law applies equally whether the victim is a son or daughter — the social status of the victim (child vs. adult) does not change the legal principle. The same ransom mechanism operates. The law applies consistently regardless of age or gender of the victim — a point worth noting against ancient legal systems where the compensation for killing a child (especially a female child) was typically far lower than for an adult male.
32: If the ox gores a male or female slave, the owner must pay thirty shekels of silver to the slave's master. Thirty shekels was the standard price of a slave in the ancient Near East — the standard is also attested in Hittite law. This is the price at which a human life (enslaved) was legally valued. The figure of thirty shekels appears three times in the OT in loaded contexts: here (slave-value), in Zechariah 11:12-13 (the prophet is contemptuously paid thirty shekels for his labor — the price of a slave — and told to throw it to the potter), and in Matthew 26:15 (Judas is paid thirty shekels to betray Jesus). The progression: ox-goring ransom for a slave → contemptuous prophet's wage → the price of the betrayal of the Son of God. The amount that begins as legal compensation ends as theological revelation.
33: The open pit law — if a man opens a pit or digs one and fails to cover it, and an ox or donkey falls in, he must compensate the animal's owner. The principle: you are responsible for hazards you create on your property (or in shared spaces) that cause harm to others. The cistern or pit is not inherently dangerous; the failure to cover it creates the danger. The law applies negligence liability to passive harm (failure to secure against foreseeable risk), not just active harm (the direct blow or the unrestrained dangerous animal).
34: He must pay its value in silver to the owner — the dead animal is his. The liable party pays the fair market value of the animal and receives the carcass (which has some value for hide, meat, and bone). The economic logic is restitutive, not punitive: restore the owner to the position he would have been in if the pit had been covered. ANE law codes show similar principles for cistern liability; the Mosaic law is consistent with the broader ancient legal tradition on this specific point.
35: If one man's ox injures another man's ox and it dies — when there is no prior negligence, the economic loss is shared equally. The owners sell the living ox and divide the proceeds, and they split the dead ox. The absence of negligence triggers cost-sharing rather than full liability: the harm was accidental; both parties bear part of the loss. The distinction between negligent harm (full liability, Exodus 21:36) and non-negligent harm (shared loss, Exodus 21:35) is a foundational principle of tort law maintained in legal systems today.
36: If the owner knew that his ox had been dangerous and did not secure it, he must pay an ox for the ox and the dead animal is his. Full restitution for foreseeable harm from a known dangerous condition — the negligence standard. The owner who knew his ox was dangerous and took no precautions bears full responsibility for the consequences. The dead animal goes to the negligent owner: the economic incentive structure reinforces the legal principle — negligence is costly, and the costs fall on the one who was negligent, not the innocent victim.
